FULL CONTENT:
![UKCRIC](/images/UKCRIC-logo.png)

# Project spotlight: Building digital twins of social systems

![Project spotlight: Building digital twins of social systems](/images/loading.png)
![Tim Yates](/images/loading.png)

**Research led by Nik Lomax, Associate Professor of Data Analytics for Population Research at the University of Leeds and a Fellow at the Alan Turing Institute for data science and artificial intelligence, is using digital twin computer simulations to model social systems.**

Created with the hardware funded by DAFNI, the computer simulations model social systems at a high resolution, both in terms of spatial scale and individual level attributes using techniques such as agent based models and microsimulation.

Within a digital twin which represents the social system, the team can investigate hypothetical policy change by running counterfactual experiments and assess how these impact on individuals, groups and spatial areas.

The DAFNI hardware fund enabled the team to develop digital twin models across a variety of applications and to rerun them easily, and for the team to collaborate in a local environment rather than to use cloud based services which can increase costs.

Professor Nik Lomax said, "Within a digital twin which represents the social system, we can investigate hypothetical policy change by running counterfactual experiments and assess how these impact on individuals, groups and spatial areas.

"Building an augmented/ virtual reality world will help policymakers to make more informed decisions. The ability to take our research outside the university, to demonstrate the digital world to policymakers, means we will be able to better visualise scenarios and impacts.”

The research team create individual level models in a wide range of research contexts and are utilising the DAFNI hardware due to the requirement for high computational power to develop and run these models.

One application of this research focuses on infrastructure development. In collaboration with a transport consultancy, different scenarios of transport infrastructure development are being assessed. Individual level models are being built and run on the DAFNI hardware which produce high resolution population and household estimates and projections which are then used as input to infrastructure demand models.

You can read the original article with Professor Nik Lomax on the [DAFNI website](https://dafni.ac.uk/dafni-hardware-at-the-university-of-leeds/).

![airplane digital twin](https://nsf-gov-resources.nsf.gov/styles/featured_content_mobile/s3/media_hub/AdobeStock_492604023-banner.jpg.webp?VersionId=EZTw4zsnsO1X7zKBSMDunx_KOEnKb8aj&itok=DGsvXezC)

# Digital twins beginning to deliver real-world benefits

Science fiction has been describing computers with impressive predictive powers for decades. The development of "digital twins" — a virtual model interacting with a physical system — has brought society a step closer to this dream.

In 2024, the National Academies of Science, Engineering and Medicine released a report co-funded by the U.S. National Science Foundation and other federal agencies titled [*Foundational Research Gaps and Future Directions for Digital Twins*](https://nap.nationalacademies.org/catalog/26894/foundational-research-gaps-and-future-directions-for-digital-twins). In this report, digital twins were described as "a set of virtual information constructs that mimics the structure, context, and behavior of a natural, engineered, or social system (or system-of-systems), is dynamically updated with data from its physical twin, has a predictive capability, and informs decisions that realize value. The bidirectional interaction between the virtual and the physical is central to the digital twin."

In plain language, digital twins are comprised of two parts: the virtual representation and the physical counterpart. The virtual representation relies on mathematical models to predict how its physical counterpart will behave. The physical side relies on sensors and other observing systems to provide real-time data for the virtual representation to utilize in its mathematical models. The models are then used to make changes to the system for the favored outcome.

![digital twin building](https://nsf-gov-resources.nsf.gov/styles/_inline_image_full_width/s3/media_hub/Texas%20A%26M-zach-twin-image-14aug2024.jpg?VersionId=I2yfZAIHr1ZjTg_3sGvnjg7FNmw172iH&itok=jgTy6J6y)

While the term digital twins first appeared in 2010, it relies on mathematical models that researchers have been developing for decades. The idea has already inspired potential future applications, such as having twins:

However, these applications are still decades away from being feasible, because researchers are still developing the mathematical models needed to match these lofty goals. Today's models are costly to develop and run and are often limited to a set of conditions. For example, instead of modeling an entire plane, current digital twins can only model and predict what occurs in the engine. But the foundation for these more aspirational goals is growing.

NSF supported much of the foundational research that has led to the promising outlook for digital twins, primarily by funding research into mathematical modeling. Recently, the agency has continued to fund research to expand the capabilities of digital twins to more and broader use cases.

### Digital Reefs

Woods Hole Oceanographic Institute received [funding from NSF](https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/advancedSearchResult?PIId=&PIFirstName=&PILastName=&PIOrganization=&PIState=&PIZip=&PICountry=&ProgOrganization=&ProgEleCode=&BooleanElement=All&ProgRefCode=&BooleanRef=All&Program=&ProgOfficer=&Keyword=%22Coral+Reef+Blue+Economy%22&AwardNumberOperator=&AwardAmount=&AwardInstrument=&ActiveAwards=true&ExpiredAwards=true&OriginalAwardDateOperator=&StartDateOperator=&ExpDateOperator=) to create [Digital Reefs](https://digitalreefs.org/), a program that uses digital twins to model how changing climate will impact reefs around the world. The program combines maps that show the topography of the ocean floor from scientific resources, surface landmarks from Google and the coral, algae and some plant life from [Allen Coral Atlas](https://allencoralatlas.org/) into a single visual map that can be accessed on any computer or phone.

![Sea Cucumbers and Coral Reef](https://nsf-gov-resources.nsf.gov/styles/_inline_image_full_width/s3/media_hub/Sea%20Cucumber_Feeding_1.png?VersionId=U3dHcXZ_Sa_OfQvfFVT2mz3WwrV3QZHM&itok=zv6u2FCc)

"The user is able to access the high-resolution temperature at 50-meter resolution. … The user can choose the depth they want to see the temperatures but can also click on specific points ... to see the specific temperatures of that specific grid cell at a specific time of their choice," explained Ann Cohen, the lead investigator for the Digital Reefs project, in a presentation to NSF employees. "It also allows the users to conduct 'what-if' scenarios."

She showed how users can run simulations to adjust topography and how it impacts the water temperature and sea life. Users can also download their data and share their simulated videos. The project started with the Palmyra Atoll coral reef in the Pacific Ocean and will expand to include other reefs and eventually be available to the public.

### Olympic swimming

Digital twins are not just used at the environmental level, though. Researchers from the University of Virginia and Massachusetts Institute of Technology [received](https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=2002265&HistoricalAwards=false) [funding](https://www.nsf.gov/awardsearch/showAward?AWD_ID=2055118&HistoricalAwards=false) that was used in designing and testing models of swimmers. These swimmers included NCAA champions, Olympic medalists and world champions.

Using sensors attached to the swimmers, the researchers were able to create digital twins of the athletes and analyze how they accelerated in the water. The digital twin models were then used to help determine flaws in posture and give the swimmers time to adjust. The adjustments allowed for tenths of seconds to be shaved off their swim time, which can make the difference in elite competitions. The researchers published an [article](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00283-024-10339-0) describing the history of swimming and their current findings in *The Mathematical Intelligencer*. 

### Resilitix AI

In addition to academic research, NSF has helped to fund small businesses through [America's Seed Fund](https://seedfund.nsf.gov/). Some of these small businesses use digital twin models, such as Resilitix Intelligence, which created Resilitix AI, a digital twin that models how hazards will impact infrastructures and communities. The twin was put to the test when [Hurricane Beryl hit Houston](https://new.nsf.gov/news/resilitix-supports-beryl-emergency-response-efforts-nsf) during the summer of 2024.

![Resilitix EmergenCITY used for near real-time monitoring of evacuation patterns in the wake of Hurricane Beryl](https://nsf-gov-resources.nsf.gov/styles/_inline_image_full_width/s3/media_hub/Resilitix1.jpg?VersionId=mJnEom2k0lhmatIXL41hy1G8Li3P1Jou&itok=1CwOacrF)

Digital twins still have a long way to go before they can fully map multifaceted, large-scale models, but they are already impacting the world, companies and even individuals. As the mathematical models and sensors that lead to digital twins' success improve, they will only become more powerful and advance our ability to predict what may come next.

## About the Author

![Eleanor Johnson headshot](https://nsf-gov-resources.nsf.gov/styles/author_bio_tablet/s3/2022-10/Bio%20Pic%20-%20Copy%20%282%29.jpg?VersionId=w3sbEbHuPFCzvPiB.OfVKy9JmA_eebK3&itok=TVh4AL80)

Eleanor earned her BS in Biology from Wittenberg University and her Ph.D. in Pharmacology from the University of Kentucky, after which she applied for the AAAS Science & Technology Policy Fellowship. She was placed as a Communication Specialist with the Science Policy & Communications team in the Office of Legislative & Public Affairs at the National Science Foundation.

# Human digital twins unlocking Society 5.0? Approaches, emerging risks and disruptions

You have full access to this [open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-science/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research) article

![](https://media.springernature.com/w72/springer-static/cover-hires/journal/10676?as=webp)

4962 Accesses

11 Citations

6 
Altmetric

[Explore all metrics](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1/metrics)

## Abstract

Industry 5.0 and Healthcare 5.0 converge towards a human centered society, having technological advancement as a lever. In Society 5.0, decentralized autonomous cities and a convergence of physical and cyberspace are the foundations of the new chapter of society’s development. The idea of creating digital replicas and legitimate representatives of human beings in cyberspace has become a pillar of digitalization. Society 5.0 introduces Human Digital Twins as a central element of Cyber Physical Systems that include human factors or are designed to interact with humans in a personalized fashion. Overall, the HDT and neighboring concepts are applied to depict how humans can be represented in a cyberspace. However, there are clear challenges in determining which human characteristics should take precedence, how much autonomy should be granted to HDTs to optimize their functionality and how to conceptualize the digital environment in which HDTs interact with various entities, including other digital agents and stakeholders with agency and decisional power. To harness similarities and differences of current approaches, we propose a classification of HDTs based on meta-characteristics and ethical implications. We discuss ethical implication by focusing on emerging risks and paradigm shifts and anchor the previous discussion in the vision for Society 5.0, questioning whether societal development relying on disruptive technologies, instead of leading to more human-centered societies might be driving humanized societies away from humanity.

Avoid common mistakes on your manuscript.

## Introduction

The Industry 4.0 paradigm is based on Smart Manufacturing where the automation of processes and overall efficiency are the drivers of the technological transformation of industrial environments. Up to a certain extent, this entailed replacing human operators with increasingly automated systems with the goal of reducing or avoiding the need for human intervention in the process (Xu et al., [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR86 "Xu, X., Lu, Y., Vogel-Heuser, B., & Wang, L. (2021). Industry 4.0 and industry 5.0 –Inception, conception and perception. Journal of Manufacturing Systems, 61, 530–535."); Wang et al., [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR82 "Wang, B., Zhou, H., Li, X., Yang, G., Zheng, P., Song, C., Wang, L., et al. (2023). Human Digital Twin in the context of industry 5.0. Robotics and Computer-Integrated Manufacturing, 85, 102626.")). However, the role of humans in Industry 5.0 is challenging the vision of Industry 4.0 by bringing the human not only to the center of the process but also developing methods to ensure that processes adapt to the human operator’s specific skills and characteristics paving the way to an industry thriving through sustainability, resilience and human centricity (Breque et al., [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR10 "Breque, M., De Nul, L., & Petridis, A. (2021). Industry 5.0: Towards a sustainable, human-centric and resilient European industry. European Commission, DG RTD. Brussels."); Kasinathan et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR36 "Kasinathan, P., Pugazhendhi, R., Elavarasan, R. M., Ramachandaramurthy, V. K., Ramanathan, V., Subramanian, S., & Alsharif, M. H. (2022). Realization of Sustainable Development Goals with Disruptive technologies by integrating industry 5.0, Society 5.0, Smart cities and villages. Sustainability, 14(22), 15258."); Mourtzis et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR49 "Mourtzis, D., Angelopoulos, J., & Panopoulos, N. (2022). A literature review of the challenges and opportunities of the transition from industry 4.0 to Society 5.0. Energies, 15(17), 6276."); Wang et al., [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR82 "Wang, B., Zhou, H., Li, X., Yang, G., Zheng, P., Song, C., Wang, L., et al. (2023). Human Digital Twin in the context of industry 5.0. Robotics and Computer-Integrated Manufacturing, 85, 102626."); Modoni & Sacco, [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR47 "Modoni, G. E., & Sacco, M. (2023). A Human Digital-Twin-based Framework Driving Human centricity towards industry 5.0. Sensors (Basel, Switzerland), 23(13), 6054.")).

Healthcare 5.0 aligns with Industry 5.0 reframing the role of humans and machines within a process or even within the same system. Human centricity and human-machine interaction and cooperation are at the grassroots. Thus, the system should be able to capture and adapt to the specific human in the loop and proactively interact or collaborate in the pursuit of goals, while fostering human well-being. In the field of healthcare, the idea of virtual patients was put forth early (Cook & Triola, [2009](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR15 "Cook, D. A., & Triola, M. M. (2009). Virtual patients: A critical literature review and proposed next steps. Medical Education, 43(4), 303–311.")) and an underlying aspect for the key characteristics of the future medicine i.e. predictive, preventive, personalized, and participatory (Hood & Flores, [2012](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR34 "Hood, L., & Flores, M. (2012). A personal view on systems medicine and the emergence of proactive P4 medicine: Predictive, preventive, personalized and participatory. New Biotechnology, 29(6), 613–624."); Bulc et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR12 "Bulc, V., Hart, B., Hannah, M., & Hrovatin, B. (2022). Society 5.0 and a human centred health care. Medicine-Based Informatics and Engineering, 147, 177.")). Personalized, precision medicine includes, for instance, a decentralization of health and wellness-related data and multiplying the possibilities to leverage such data to deliver personalized healthcare and health-related advice (Bagaria et al., [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR3 "Bagaria, N., Laamarti, F., Badawi, H. F., Albraikan, A., Martinez Velazquez, R. A., & Saddik, E. (2020). A. Health 4.0: Digital twins for health and well-being. Connected Health in Smart Cities, 143–152."); Turab & Jamil, [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR77 "Turab, M., & Jamil, S. (2023). A Comprehensive Survey of Digital Twins in Healthcare in the era of Metaverse. BioMedInformatics, 3(3), 563–584.")) and a way forward to democratizing health data (Demetriou et al., [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR18 "Demetriou, D., Mathabe, K., Lolas, G., & Dlamini, Z. (2023). Internet of things in Society 5.0 and the Democratization of Healthcare. Society 5.0 and Next Generation Healthcare: Patient-focused and technology-assisted Precision therapies (pp. 111–130). Springer Nature Switzerland.")). As the industry and healthcare are steering towards a new paradigm, a new vision for society is on the rise – one fully embracing AI in all human activities and progressively merging all aspects of physical space and cyberspace. Society 5.0[Footnote 1](#Fn1) envisions a new chapter of societal development. Society 4.0 (information society) raised in response to the uptake of Information and Communication Technologies (ICT), leading to networked cities driven by profitability. Society 5.0 focuses on humanity and relies on ubiquitous data collection and decentralized access to data, using AI-based systems to mediate and engage humans in continuous and synchronous interactions with and within cyberspace towards full cyber-physical convergence (Gladden, [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR28 "Gladden, M. E. (2019). Who will be the members of Society 5.0? Towards an anthropology of technologically posthumanized future societies. Social Sciences, 8(5), 148."); H-UTokyo Lab, [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR33 "Hitachi-UTokyo Laboratory (H-UTokyo. (2020). Lab), Society 5.0: A people-Centric Super-smart Society. Springer Open.")).

As put forward by the Japanese government and further developed by the Japanese scientific community, the underlying value of humanity is based on the idea of human co-becoming (H-UTokyo Lab, [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR33 "Hitachi-UTokyo Laboratory (H-UTokyo. (2020). Lab), Society 5.0: A people-Centric Super-smart Society. Springer Open.")). To fulfill humanity, humans should be able to engage with the environment and other beings in an emphatic and collaborative way. This should be possible by shifting the focus of development from economic-driven values to human-centric ones, including individual wellbeing and collective harmony. A nonmonetary and data-driven society are envisioned as the baseline to untap the true value of people as creators of solutions to social problems, given that all would be equally able to have high-quality lives and access meaningful information to augment physical and mental capacities as well as the ability to make informed decisions (H-UTokyo Lab, [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR33 "Hitachi-UTokyo Laboratory (H-UTokyo. (2020). Lab), Society 5.0: A people-Centric Super-smart Society. Springer Open."); Beniiche et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR7 "Beniiche, A., Rostami, S., & Maier, M. (2022). Society 5.0: Internet as if people mattered. IEEE Wireless Communications, 29(6), 160–168.")).

While the concept of Digital Twins was driving Industry 4.0, the new focus on human centricity makes the HDTs a central piece for the alignment of technology with human values. HDT can be defined as a conceptual framework that enables channels and combines methods aiming at placing human beings as peers of other humans and artificial agents in cyberspace, towards full convergence of physical and cyberspace within complex adaptive systems and urging the emergence of digital selves (Barachini & Stary, [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR4 "Barachini, F., & Stary, C. (2022). From digital twins to digital selves and beyond: Engineering and social models for a trans-humanist world. Springer Nature.")). Indeed, by placing humans at the center of every system bridging physical and cyberspace, HDTs might be the key to unlock the aforementioned visions for the future of health, industry and society.

However, forcing a paradigm shift in societies is not without challenges for individuals and communities. Advancements in technology have proved to introduce or aggravate social vulnerabilities and generate imbalances in power structures and in relation to the sustainability of human footprint on Earth. Furthermore, the visions driving change under the claim of human centricity are not bringing completely new values to the table, so the question is why we should expect that this time around society would actually thrive in the pursuit of sustainable development and how HDT are the missing piece to achieve social cohesion and the common good or rather will introduce more challenges to human coexistence with other beings.

Indeed, the concept of HDT is still ill defined and the first attempt to consolidate an unified HDT is recent (Miller & Spatz, [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR46 "Miller, M. E., & Spatz, E. (2022). A unified view of a human digital twin. Human-Intelligent Systems Integration, 4(1–2), 23–33.")). However, neighboring concepts have sprouted in the last decade, stemming from diverse fields of research and in response to specific objectives. These include, for instance, personal digital twin (Nativi et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR52 "Nativi, S., Craglia, M., & Sciullo, L. (2022). MyDigitalTwin: Exploratory Research Report (No. JRC129917). Joint Research Centre (Seville site).")), virtual patient (Westerhoff et al., [2008](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR83 "Westerhoff, H. V., Mosekilde, E., Noe, C. R., & Clemensen, A. M. (2008). Integrating systems approaches into pharmaceutical sciences. European Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 35(1–2), 1–4."); Cook & Triola, [2009](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR15 "Cook, D. A., & Triola, M. M. (2009). Virtual patients: A critical literature review and proposed next steps. Medical Education, 43(4), 303–311.")), home assistant (Rafailidis & Manolopoulos, [2018](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR59 "Rafailidis, D., & Manolopoulos, Y. (2018). The technological gap between virtual assistants and recommendation systems. arXiv preprint arXiv:1901.00431.")), personal digital assistant (Maedche et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR40 "Maedche, A., Legner, C., Benlian, A., Berger, B., Gimpel, H., Hess, T., & Söllner, M. (2019). AI-based digital assistants: Opportunities, threats, and research perspectives. Business & Information Systems Engineering, 61, 535–544."); Kerckhove, [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR37 "De Kerckhove, D. (2021). The personal digital twin, ethical considerations. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 379(2207), 20200367.")), cognitive digital twins (Fernández et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR24 "Fernández, F., Sánchez, Á., Vélez, J. F., & Moreno, A. B. (2019). Symbiotic autonomous systems with consciousness using digital twins. In From Bioinspired Systems and Biomedical Applications to Machine Learning: 8th International Work-Conference on the Interplay Between Natural and Artificial Computation, IWINAC 2019, Almería, Spain, June 3–7, 2019, Proceedings, Part II 8 (pp. 23–32). Springer International Publishing."); Somers et al., [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR71 "Somers, S., Oltramari, A., & Lebiere, C. (2020, March). Cognitive Twin: A Cognitive Approach to Personalized Assistants. In AAAI Spring Symposium: Combining Machine Learning with Knowledge Engineering (1)."); Barachini & Stary, [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR4 "Barachini, F., & Stary, C. (2022). From digital twins to digital selves and beyond: Engineering and social models for a trans-humanist world. Springer Nature.")), algorithm of you and prolifing algoritthms (De Vries, [2010](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR80 "de Vries, K. (2010). Identity, profiling algorithms and a world of ambient intelligence. Ethics and Information Technology, 12, 71–85."); Peltz, [2017](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR57 "Peltz, J. J. (2017). The Algorithm of you: Your Profile of Preference or an Agent for Evil? Naval War College."); Kerckhove, [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR37 "De Kerckhove, D. (2021). The personal digital twin, ethical considerations. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 379(2207), 20200367."); Mallinger et al., [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR41 "Mallinger, K., Schatten, A., Sendera, G., Klemen, M., & Tjoa, A. M. (2021). Potential threats of Human Digital Twins for Digital Sovereignty and the Sustainable Development Goals. UN IATT Report."); Teller, [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR75 "Teller, M. (2021). Legal aspects related to digital twin. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 379(2207), 20210023.")). In this paper, we question whether these concepts are related enough to go under the HDT umbrella and propose a classification of HDTs based on types. To measure similarities and differences, we identified meta-characteristics and ethical implications and present a cross-examination of HDT types using the meta-characteristics and ethical implications as dimensions. In extension, we discuss the ethical implication of HDTs by addressing emerging risks and paradigm shifts, while questioning whether societal development relying on HDTs and physical-cyber convergence is promoting human-centered societies or rather driving humanized societies away from humanity.

We start by presenting how HDTs can be the key to unlocking several aspects of Healthcare 5.0, Industry 5.0 and Society 5.0 (Sect. [Future societies: the rise and role of human digital twins](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#Sec2)). The next section addresses the definition of HDT based on emerging approaches and neighboring concepts that were put forward in the past, in order to infer meta-characteristics setting up a baseline to discuss the existence of various HDT types (Sect. [Definitional problems and completeness](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#Sec7)). We present a taxonomy for HDT grounded in an extensive review of existing literature. This classification delineates four distinct types, taking into consideration both meta-characteristics and ethical implications (Sect. [HDT types: intertwined concepts and pervasiveness](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#Sec12)). Subsequently, we engage in an analytical discourse concerning the ethical challenges and examine the transformative potential of this technology within the context of an emergent, post-humanized society (Sect. [Human Digital Twins in Future Society](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#Sec19)).We conclude by addressing the meanings of HDTs for a novel technologically post-humanized society.

## Future societies: the rise and role of human digital twins

Transporting the concept of Digital Twins from objects and closed manufacturing processes to humans has become increasingly visible in the literature, often addressed from the lenses of specific sectors or use cases, but also towards a unified view for the HDT concept (Miller & Spatz, [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR46 "Miller, M. E., & Spatz, E. (2022). A unified view of a human digital twin. Human-Intelligent Systems Integration, 4(1–2), 23–33.")). Nonetheless, the idea of creating models or digital replicas of humans for different purposes is far from new. Virtual Patients have been proposed in several fields of medicine and pharmaceutical sciences (Westerhoff et al., [2008](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR83 "Westerhoff, H. V., Mosekilde, E., Noe, C. R., & Clemensen, A. M. (2008). Integrating systems approaches into pharmaceutical sciences. European Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences, 35(1–2), 1–4."); Cook & Triola, [2009](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR15 "Cook, D. A., & Triola, M. M. (2009). Virtual patients: A critical literature review and proposed next steps. Medical Education, 43(4), 303–311.")). Home assistants are already adapting to the user’s preferences, while combining virtual assistants with recommendation systems has been identified as one prospective avenue for research (Rafailidis & Manolopoulos, [2018](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR59 "Rafailidis, D., & Manolopoulos, Y. (2018). The technological gap between virtual assistants and recommendation systems. arXiv preprint arXiv:1901.00431.")) towards personal digital assistants (Maedche et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR40 "Maedche, A., Legner, C., Benlian, A., Berger, B., Gimpel, H., Hess, T., & Söllner, M. (2019). AI-based digital assistants: Opportunities, threats, and research perspectives. Business & Information Systems Engineering, 61, 535–544."); Kerckhove, [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR37 "De Kerckhove, D. (2021). The personal digital twin, ethical considerations. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 379(2207), 20200367.")). Another strand of research proposes cognitive digital twins as possible frameworks to endow systems with representations of human-like cognitive processes and knowledge (Fernández et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR24 "Fernández, F., Sánchez, Á., Vélez, J. F., & Moreno, A. B. (2019). Symbiotic autonomous systems with consciousness using digital twins. In From Bioinspired Systems and Biomedical Applications to Machine Learning: 8th International Work-Conference on the Interplay Between Natural and Artificial Computation, IWINAC 2019, Almería, Spain, June 3–7, 2019, Proceedings, Part II 8 (pp. 23–32). Springer International Publishing."); Somers et al., [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR71 "Somers, S., Oltramari, A., & Lebiere, C. (2020, March). Cognitive Twin: A Cognitive Approach to Personalized Assistants. In AAAI Spring Symposium: Combining Machine Learning with Knowledge Engineering (1)."); Barachini & Stary, [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR4 "Barachini, F., & Stary, C. (2022). From digital twins to digital selves and beyond: Engineering and social models for a trans-humanist world. Springer Nature.")).

### Human digital twins in Cyber-physical systems (CPS)

Although many existing systems claim to embed human centricity, there is still much room to explore how human-centric approaches can and should be enabled. For instance, human centricity in CPSs is perceived as having the human in the loop represented in the CPS. The objectives are both to improve the overall quality of the system (i.e. its performance) to achieve specific goals at an optimized level and to enable an alignment with human values (Helbing et al., [2021](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR30 "Helbing, D., Fanitabasi, F., Giannotti, F., Hänggli, R., van den Hausladen, C. I., Pournaras, E., et al. (2021). Ethics of smart cities: Towards value-sensitive design and co-evolving city life. Sustainability, 13(20), 11162.")) and needs at different levels, namely at the level of the individual (Sparrow et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR69 "Sparrow, D., Kruger, K., & Basson, A. (2019). Human digital twin for integrating human workers in industry 4.0. In Proceedings of the International Conference on Competitive Manufacturing. Stellenbosch, South Africa."); Wang et al., [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR82 "Wang, B., Zhou, H., Li, X., Yang, G., Zheng, P., Song, C., Wang, L., et al. (2023). Human Digital Twin in the context of industry 5.0. Robotics and Computer-Integrated Manufacturing, 85, 102626.")).

To this end, both Human-Cyber-Physical Systems (HCPS) and Cyber-Physical-Social Systems (CPSS) present a strong focus on capturing human factors, which are then taken into account while adapting the systems in relation to specific characteristics of an individual or leveraging the data to create collective value. However, in an HCPS there is not always an intention of enabling a direct interaction with the human represented in the systems; instead, some use cases care to capture and embed human aspects in the system under different purposes. Another fundamental difference lies in the overall goals of the human-machine interaction either branching in the direction of centralizing and deepening knowledge around a specific individual or group of individuals or expanding the scope towards decentralized forms of social interactions (Zhou et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR87 "Zhou, Y., Yu, F. R., Chen, J., & Kuo, Y. (2019). Cyber-physical-social systems: A state-of-the-art survey, challenges and opportunities. IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials, 22(1), 389–425."); Shangguan et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR66 "Shangguan, D., Chen, L., Su, C., Ding, J., & Liu, C. (2022). A Triple Human-Digital Twin Architecture for Cyber-physical systems. CMES-Computer Modeling in Engineering & Sciences, 131(3).")).

When direct interaction is considered in HCPSs, individuals can experience an augmentation of their capacities while collaborating in a more intuitive way with artificial agents and robots. This can apply to both physical and cognitive capacities. Alternatively, the representation of humans in HCPSs can be considered merely to generate knowledge on the individual and thus, limited access and control over the system and model is given to the twinned individual(s) (Wang et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR81 "Wang, B., Zhou, H., Yang, G., Li, X., & Yang, H. (2022). Human digital twin (HDT) driven human-cyber-physical systems: Key technologies and applications. Chinese Journal of Mechanical Engineering, 35(1), 11.")).

CPSSs entail the integration of social systems within CPSs. The objective is to establish decentralized collaborations and interactions under specific rules inherent to the system. By cooperating to complete specific tasks, individuals are simultaneously contributing to the overall functioning of the system. In this sense, individuals are not only a service’s end users, they cumulatively generate value for the community (Zhou et al., [2019](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR87 "Zhou, Y., Yu, F. R., Chen, J., & Kuo, Y. (2019). Cyber-physical-social systems: A state-of-the-art survey, challenges and opportunities. IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials, 22(1), 389–425."); Beniiche et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR7 "Beniiche, A., Rostami, S., & Maier, M. (2022). Society 5.0: Internet as if people mattered. IEEE Wireless Communications, 29(6), 160–168.")).

### Human digital twins and hyper-personalization

Hyper-personalization or hyper-customization bring closer together the ideas of customization and personalization under the objective of expanding the level of information available on the end user to deliver hyper-tailored services or products (Mourtzis et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR49 "Mourtzis, D., Angelopoulos, J., & Panopoulos, N. (2022). A literature review of the challenges and opportunities of the transition from industry 4.0 to Society 5.0. Energies, 15(17), 6276.")). In this scenario, the interaction of end users and organizations would no longer require an individual’s informational input; instead providing suppliers with one’s unique traits becomes an externalized process through data and data processing/sharing techniques. Customization allows filtering a set of objects or choices provided by an organization to obtain tailored options or information, while personalization results in a supplier creating a specific good or service for an end user, given their unique characteristics and markers.

At a broader level, personalization is one of the pivotal aspects for human centricity in cyber-physical systems. The HDT and neighboring concepts are addressed as frameworks for advancing the personalization of healthcare (Bruynseels et al., [2018](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR11 "Bruynseels, K., de Sio, S., F., & n den Hoven, J. (2018). Digital twins in health care: Ethical implications of an emerging engineering paradigm. Frontiers in Genetics, 9, 31."); Bagaria et al., [2020](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR3 "Bagaria, N., Laamarti, F., Badawi, H. F., Albraikan, A., Martinez Velazquez, R. A., & Saddik, E. (2020). A. Health 4.0: Digital twins for health and well-being. Connected Health in Smart Cities, 143–152."); Männikkö et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR43 "Männikkö, V., Kopponen, A., Mikkonen, T., Förger, K., & Torhola, M. (2022). Strengthening Well-Being Based on National Health Records: Citizen Digital Twins with Personal Risk Assessment. Available at SSRN 4181368."); Okegbile et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR56 "Okegbile, S. D., Cai, J., Yi, C., & Niyato, D. (2022). Human digital twin for personalized healthcare: Vision, architecture and future directions. IEEE network."); Sahal et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR61 "Sahal, R., Alsamhi, S. H., & Brown, K. N. (2022). Personal digital twin: A close look into the present and a step towards the future of personalised healthcare industry. Sensors (Basel, Switzerland), 22(15), 5918.")) and education (Furini et al., [2022](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR27 "Furini, M., Gaggi, O., Mirri, S., Montangero, M., Pelle, E., Poggi, F., & Prandi, C. (2022). Digital twins and artificial intelligence: As pillars of personalized learning models. Communications of the ACM, 65(4), 98–104."); Arantes, [2023](/article/10.1007/s10676-024-09787-1#ref-CR2 "Arantes, J. (2023). Digital twins and the terminology of personalization or personalized learning in educational policy: A discussion paper. Policy Futures in Education.")). This entails that information is filtered and presented in a tailored fashion. At the same time, the system should be able to portray and predict the individual’s needs, health condition, behavior and cognitive processes in an accurate and adaptive manner so that it continuously delivers t

enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital twins enable real-time modeling of social interactions, facilitating scenario analysis and policy testing, thus revolutionizing how societies adapt and evolve.
By simulating various social dynamics, digital twins help tackle issues like urban planning, healthcare access, and resource distribution, providing data-driven insights for effective solutions.
Digital twins promote sustainable practices by enabling precise resource management and environmental impact assessments, aligning societal growth with ecological preservation.
